title: Riven oak chair leg
subtitle: Riving and shaping a tapered chair leg from a green oak round
excerpt: A riven chair leg has grain fibres running unbroken from tenon to foot, making it several times stronger than a sawn equivalent of the same dimensions. The leg is split from a straight-grained round with a froe, rough-shaped with a side axe, and brought to its taper and final round section on the shaving horse with a drawknife and spokeshave. This advanced project covers the full riving sequence, reading grain under the drawknife, and the taper ratios used in the traditional stick-chair.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for riven-oak chair-making. Jenkin, The Agricultural and Forest Industries of Wales (1901) for the Welsh stick-chair leg dimensions and tenon ratios.

content.text: Every Windsor chair, stick chair, and bodger's stool sits on legs that were
riven

rather than sawn. The practical consequence is that a leg no thicker than 30 mm can carry the weight and repeated shock loading of a person sitting, standing from, and rocking on a chair, without breaking at the grain-exit point that would cause a sawn leg to snap. A four-legged chair needs four matched legs; the sequence below covers one. Work a matched set of
billets
through each step together rather than completing one leg at a time; the consistency of matched sets is easier to achieve by passing all four through each stage in sequence.
What you need: wood

A straight green oak round, 45 cm long, 10 to 12 cm in diameter, from the main stem below the first branch. Look for straight grain lines on the side and concentric growth rings on the end; spiral grain will produce a leg that twists during drying and may be unusable. One round gives four to six leg blanks depending on diameter.
What you need: tools
A froe and beetle to rive the blanks. A side axe on a side-axe block for the initial rough shaping. A
drawknife
for the main taper work. A spokeshave for the final round section. A
shaving horse
throughout. An India oilstone and leather strop; green oak dulls a drawknife edge faster than most other species.
Safety

Keep feet behind the froe and beetle strike zone. On the shaving horse, keep the foot pedal firmly depressed throughout each drawknife pass; releasing it lets the billet shift while the blade is in contact.
The work
Step 1: Rive the leg blanks

Stand the round on end and drive the froe across the diameter with the beetle. Split each half into quarters. From each quarter, rive off the outer sapwood if present; sapwood in oak is softer than heartwood and weakens a chair leg. Each finished billet is a roughly triangular section about 50 mm across, with grain running end to end along its full length.
Step 2: Side-axe to rough octagon

Rest each billet on the side-axe block. Score relief cuts across the billet every 5 cm, then hew between them to remove the three corners of the triangular section to a rough octagon. The target section at this stage is 42 mm across the flats at the tenon end, tapering to 28 mm at the foot. Keep the side-axe flat face outward on every stroke; if the bevel face is outward, the axe wedges rather than slices.
Step 3: Drawknife to taper

Clamp the billet in the shaving horse with the tenon end away from the operator. Pull the drawknife from foot toward tenon, following the grain. Green oak produces long continuous shavings when cut with the grain; if shavings are short and the surface tears, reverse direction. Bring the section to a smooth octagon maintaining the taper from top to foot. Hone the drawknife on the India oilstone every fifteen passes; oak tannins blunt the edge rapidly.
Step 4: Spokeshave to round section

Switch to the spokeshave and work the octagonal billet to a consistent round section in overlapping passes, rotating the billet a quarter-turn between passes. The target section is 34 mm at the tenon end tapering to 22 mm at the foot over 42 cm. After the spokeshave, cut the tenon with the sloyd knife: a round tenon, 22 mm diameter, 50 mm long, tapered from the leg round to the tenon diameter.
Variations
Ash legs are lighter than oak and slightly more flexible, which absorbs shock better in a dining chair used daily. Traditional Windsor undercarriages used ash for this reason.
A baluster leg is thicker at the centre and tapered at both ends. Use the same riving and drawknife steps. Start with a longer billet (50 cm) and shape from both ends toward the centre.
Care
Chair legs riven in green oak require no finish before assembly into a seat. If left as spare blanks, coat with boiled linseed oil and store in a ventilated shed until needed.

attrs.intro: Problems in riving and shaping chair legs.

items.fix: Reverse the working direction and hone on the India oilstone. Plan to hone every fifteen passes in green oak regardless of perceived sharpness.
items.cause: Cutting against the grain, or the edge is dull from the oak tannins.
items.symptom: The drawknife tears the surface rather than cutting clean shavings

items.fix: Re-set the froe on the face of the split at the point of deviation and drive again. If the grain spirals through more than 5 degrees over the length of the leg, the log is not suitable for riven chair legs.
items.cause: Spiral grain in the log, or the froe was not re-set on the riven face to correct the angle.
items.symptom: The rive deviates from the axis after the first 15 to 20 cm

attrs.heading: Common problems
